Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a active Pacific system day — a deepening low-pressure complex is moving into the Pacific Northwest by Monday afternoon, bringing a cold frontal passage directly across the KBDN–KSQL corridor during your planned flight window. Primary Risk: frontal-embedded IMC, mountain obscuration over the Cascades/Sierras, and wind-over-terrain turbulence at 12,000 ft as the system crosses Oregon and Northern California during your departure and en route window. MARGINAL GO at best, trending NO-GO — the system timing puts the frontal boundary very close to your departure and en route window, and the 48-hour prog shows the low at ~1004 mb over the Pacific Northwest at Mon 12Z, only hours before your 21:30Z departure. Single most important trigger: frontal passage timing relative to your 21:30Z departure — if the front is still over Oregon/Northern California at departure time, this becomes NO-GO.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

  • Sunday evening (29 Mar, ~06Z–12Z): Pull updated TAFs for KBDN, KSQL, KRDM (Redmond as alternate), KSJC (San Jose as alternate), and KSFO. Look for ceilings trending below 3,000 ft BKN or OVC and visibility below 5 SM at either end.
  • Frontal timing is everything: The 48-hr prog (Mon 12Z) shows a deep low centered over Idaho/Montana with the cold front sagging into Oregon and Northern California. If Sunday evening model runs are pushing that front's arrival at KBDN later than 00Z Monday night/Tuesday, your window improves. If the front is shown arriving at or before 18Z Monday, reconsider departure entirely.
  • QPF flag: The Day 2 42–48hr QPF (valid Mon 06Z) and Day 3 24-hr total both show significant precip in the Pacific Northwest and extending into northern California. Check if the QPF bullseye shifts south toward the KBDN–KSQL corridor overnight Sunday.
  • Check for SIGMETs/G-AIRMETs: Look specifically for mountain obscuration, IFR conditions over the Cascades and northern Sierras, and any airframe icing AIRMETs for the 10,000–14,000 ft band over Oregon/California.
  • Go/No-Go trigger for the night-before check: If updated model guidance shows the 1004–1008 mb low center within 200 nm of KBDN at your departure time, or if TAFs show ceilings below 2,000 ft OVC at KBDN with IFR conditions, stand down and reassess in the morning.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-30 21:30Z

KBDN sits at 3,459 ft MSL in the high desert east of the Cascades. In pre-frontal or frontal conditions, the picture can change rapidly. Based on the 48-hr and 60-hr surface progs, here is what to expect at 21:30Z Monday:

  • The cold front is likely positioned near or just west/northwest of Bend by departure time, with the low center over the Pacific Northwest interior. This puts KBDN in pre-frontal or frontal transition — expect increasing clouds, possible ceilings dropping to 3,000–5,000 ft AGL, and wind shifting from southerly to westerly as the front pushes through.
  • Precipitation: The QPF Day2 42–48hr panel shows light QPF touching the Pacific Northwest, with the heavier precip focused over western Oregon and the Cascades. KBDN may see light rain showers or rain/snow mix in pre-frontal moisture, but significant precipitation at KBDN is possible rather than likely.
  • Departure assessment: This is potentially a brief layer penetration to IMC departure scenario — not a clear VMC departure. Ceilings may be marginal VFR (2,000–3,500 ft AGL) or broken IMC depending on frontal timing. Plan for an IFR departure. Confirm winds: pre-frontal southerlies favor Runway 16; post-frontal westerlies may create a crosswind situation on the 16/34 runway.
  • KBDN is non-towered, hours 0800–1700 local. Your 21:30Z departure is approximately 1:30 PM local (PDT), so the FBO will be open, but get a weather briefing before engine start.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Winds Aloft

No winds aloft data was provided for this specific flight. Based on the synoptic pattern from the surface progs at flight time (Mon ~21Z):

  • A deepening low over the Pacific Northwest with tightly packed isobars implies strong southwesterly flow at altitude over Oregon and Northern California — likely 30–50 kt at 12,000 ft from 210–240°.
  • This translates to a headwind component of 20–40 kt on the KBDN–KSQL track (roughly 195° true), which runs nearly into the flow. Expect a slower groundspeed than typical — plan for headwind fuel burn.
  • Get actual winds aloft forecast (FB Winds) for Monday departure — the 12Z and 18Z Monday FB charts will give you actual decoded values for RDM, MFR, OAK, and SFO waypoints.

Ride Quality

Based on the synoptic setup — deep low, active frontal system, Cascade/Sierra crossing:

  • Cascades crossing (departure climb, approximately 4,500–7,000 ft AGL above terrain): With pre-frontal or frontal flow at 30–50 kt across the volcanic terrain, moderate turbulence is likely, with possible severe turbulence isolated in rotor zones lee of the higher peaks (Three Sisters, Broken Top area). This is the worst section of the flight.
  • Central Oregon/Northern California at 12,000 ft: Light to moderate turbulence likely in the frontal zone. If flying post-frontal, expect gusty but potentially smoother post-frontal airmass. If flying in the frontal boundary itself, expect moderate turbulence and embedded convective cells possible.
  • Bay Area approach: Marine layer interaction with post-frontal flow — light chop to smooth below 5,000 ft once clear of terrain.

Wind-over-terrain assessment: With estimated 35–50 kt flow at 12,000 ft crossing the Cascades, mountain wave and moderate-or-greater turbulence is likely over the Cascade crest. This is not a casual crossing.

Icing

Altitude Icing Probability Notes
Surface–6,000 ft MSL Low–Moderate (conditional) Below freezing level in pre-frontal/frontal precip; mixed rain/snow at terrain level east of Cascades
6,000–10,000 ft MSL Moderate (possible) Active frontal cloud deck with moisture; temperatures likely -5°C to -10°C; rime icing in clouds
10,000–14,000 ft MSL (cruise) Moderate to High (likely in cloud) Frontal cirrus and mid-level cloud at cruise altitude; temps likely -10°C to -18°C; mixed rime/clear icing in stratiform cloud; this is your worst icing band
Above 14,000 ft Low Above most frontal moisture; cirrus only
  • Freezing level: Estimated 6,000–8,000 ft MSL in pre-frontal airmass; may drop to near terrain post-frontal over the Cascades.
  • Worst icing band: 8,000–13,000 ft MSL in frontal cloud deck over the Cascades and northern Sierras — exactly your cruise altitude.
  • SLD risk: No explicit SLD signal from available data. However, with active frontal precipitation containing both rain and snow phases in the lower layers, SLD cannot be ruled out in the 6,000–10,000 ft band where temperatures hover near 0°C. Explicitly no SLD SIGMET in available data, but absence of a SIGMET is not a clearance.
  • Critical point: If you are not in a certified known-ice aircraft, this icing environment at cruise altitude is a hard stop.

Altitude Strategy

  • Best altitude band: If the frontal system has passed and you're in the post-frontal airmass, descending to 8,000–10,000 ft (below the worst icing band, above most terrain except the Cascade crest) may offer a better ride with reduced icing. However, this brings terrain clearance closer over the Cascades — file IFR and confirm MEAs.
  • Worst altitude band: 10,000–13,000 ft in the frontal cloud deck — maximum icing exposure with frontal turbulence. Your planned 12,000 ft puts you in this zone.
  • Escape option: If icing accumulation is significant and turbulence is moderate or greater, your options are:
    1. Climb above 14,000 ft (above icing, but requires oxygen and may worsen turbulence near the tropopause)
    2. Divert to KRDM (Redmond) or KMFR (Medford) if conditions deteriorate over Oregon before crossing the Cascades
    3. Turn around to KBDN if conditions deteriorate shortly after departure — the Cascades are your point of no comfortable return

Hazard Table

Hazard Risk Level Leg What to Expect
Frontal IMC / low ceilings High (likely) Departure + Cascade crossing Broken to overcast ceilings, possibly below 3,000 ft AGL at KBDN; solid IMC over Cascades
Airframe icing (rime/mixed) High (likely) in frontal cloud Cascades + Northern CA Icing in cloud 8,000–13,000 ft; worst over Cascade crest; requires known-ice capability
Mountain wave / turbulence Moderate–High (likely) Cascade crossing Moderate turbulence likely, severe possible in wave rotors; 30–50 kt flow across terrain
Frontal turbulence Moderate (likely) En route OR/CA Light to moderate chop in frontal zone; possibly smooth in post-frontal clear airmass
Mountain obscuration High (likely) Cascade crest Peaks and terrain obscured in cloud; IFR required; terrain clearance critical
Strong headwinds Moderate (likely) Full route Estimated 20–40 kt headwind component; significant fuel burn increase; plan fuel reserves
Bay Area marine layer Low–Moderate (possible) KSQL arrival Post-frontal onshore flow may compress marine layer; ceilings 800–1,500 ft possible at KSQL

Getting Into KSQL

KSQL is a short, sea-level runway (2,621 ft, elevation 5.5 ft MSL) at San Carlos, tucked between San Francisco Bay to the east and the Santa Cruz Mountains to the southwest. It's towered during your expected arrival window (~23:50Z = approximately 4:50 PM PDT), so tower will be open.

  • Estimated block time: KBDN to KSQL is approximately 280–300 nm direct. At 12,000 ft with an estimated 25–35 kt average headwind component, expect 2.5–3.5 hours depending on actual winds. Arrival approximately 23:50Z–01:00Z Tuesday (4:50–6:00 PM PDT Monday).
  • Arrival conditions: Post-frontal onshore flow hitting the Bay Area. The QPF shows precip clearing the Bay Area by Monday afternoon/evening, but post-frontal westerlies commonly compress the marine layer against the coastal hills. KSQL ceilings of 1,000–2,500 ft BKN or OVC are possible, especially if the post-frontal airmass is moist. This is a conditional concern — not a certainty.
  • Runway wind estimate: Post-frontal westerly flow at the surface typically gives KSQL winds from 280–310°. Runway 30 (300°) would be favored — crosswind on Runway 12, headwind on Runway 30. Expect gusts 15–25 kt in post-frontal conditions. Uncertainty is high — Bay Area post-frontal winds can be gusty and variable in speed.
  • Terrain note: The approach to Runway 30 has a 100-ft steel transmission tower and powerlines 750 ft west and parallel to the runway, plus a 7-ft lighted dike in the runway safety area on the approach end of Runway 30. In low visibility or gusty crosswind conditions, these obstacles are operationally significant. The runway is also right traffic Runway 30. Know your approach briefing cold before you get there.
  • Practical alternates:
    • KSJC (San Jose): Primary alternate — longer runways, ILS approaches, fuller instrument capability. 8 nm southeast of KSQL.
    • KSFO (San Francisco): Secondary alternate — full ILS, 24-hour tower, full ATC support. 12 nm northwest.
    • KHAF (Half Moon Bay): Closer to KSQL, but also subject to marine layer — less useful as alternate in marine layer conditions.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — with significant conditions attached. Trending toward NO-GO depending on morning-of update.

This is not a benign flight. The synoptic setup for Monday afternoon is an active Pacific frontal system arriving over Oregon, putting the departure airport in frontal transition and the en route corridor in IMC with icing and terrain turbulence. The primary risks — icing at cruise altitude, mountain wave over the Cascades, and frontal IMC — are all simultaneously present along this route.

Hard stops:

  • Aircraft is not certified for known icing — this is a NO-GO. Full stop. Icing at cruise altitude is likely, not just possible.
  • Frontal boundary still positioned over the Cascades at departure time — NO-GO until passage confirmed.
  • KBDN ceilings below 1,500 ft OVC at departure time without IFR capability — NO-GO.
  • KSQL reporting IFR conditions and both KSJC and KSFO below alternate minimums simultaneously — NO-GO.

Watch items (conditional go):

  • Frontal system tracks faster than modeled — if the front clears Oregon by 18Z Monday, post-frontal conditions may allow a later Monday evening departure in clearing airmass.
  • Icing G-AIRMET or SIGMET covering the Cascades at cruise altitude — downgrade to NO-GO if moderate icing SIGMET is issued.

What changes this call

  • Frontal passage timing: If the cold front clears KBDN by 18Z Monday (3 hours before departure), post-frontal conditions may produce acceptable VFR/MVFR. If it's still over Oregon at 21Z, NO-GO.
  • Winds at 12,000 ft: If Monday's FB winds show greater than 45 kt at 12,000 ft over the Cascades, mountain wave severity increases substantially — reconsider altitude or delay until winds moderate.
  • Icing SIGMET issued for the Cascade/northern California corridor at cruise altitude — immediate NO-GO regardless of other factors.
  • KSQL/KSJC both below 1,000 ft OVC at estimated arrival time — no viable alternate exists, NO-GO.

If You Go — Do This

  1. File IFR. This flight in this weather environment requires IFR capability and a certified known-ice aircraft. There is no practical VFR routing over the Cascades in frontal conditions.
  2. Fuel: Plan for 35–40 kt average headwind at cruise. Add at minimum 45 minutes fuel beyond your reserve on top of standard IFR reserves. Carry fuel to KSJC as your filed alternate.
  3. Altitude: Request 12,000 ft but brief your escape plan — if icing is significant, either climb above 14,000 ft (have oxygen) or request lower and accept terrain proximity. Know your MEAs on the IFR route over the Cascades cold.
  4. Departure window: Best window: wait for frontal passage confirmation before departing. If the front clears KBDN by early afternoon Monday, a 22:30Z–23:00Z departure (15:30–16:00 PDT) may catch the post-frontal clearing while still arriving KSQL before marine layer redevelops overnight. Worst window: 20:00–21:30Z Monday — likely still in the frontal boundary with maximum IMC/icing exposure.
  5. Morning of (Monday 28 March, ~14Z): Pull updated TAFs (should now be valid for your flight window), PIREPs over Oregon and the Cascades, active G-AIRMETs for IFR/mountain obscuration/icing, and updated FB winds for the 12Z and 18Z Monday run. Reassess go/no-go with fresh data.
  6. KBDN operational note: Non-towered, file for IFR release via Seattle Center at 253-351-3694. Call ahead — don't assume you can get a void time easily from the ramp.
  7. KSQL arrival: Brief the Runway 30 obstacles (100-ft towers, 7-ft dike on approach end) before descent. If winds are gusty, consider diverting to KSJC where longer runways and better instrument approaches provide more margin.

Confidence

Low-to-Medium confidence overall.

  • This briefing is based on 48–60 hour surface progs — at the outer edge of useful synoptic precision for frontal timing. Model agreement on the system's existence is high; frontal arrival timing at KBDN has an uncertainty window of ±4–6 hours, which at your departure time is operationally significant.
  • No winds aloft data was provided — wind estimates at cruise altitude are inferred from the synoptic pattern, not decoded from actual FB charts. Get the actual Monday FB winds before flying.
  • No TAFs were available for the flight window — both KBDN and KSQL TAF data was flagged as not yet covering flight time. Terminal forecasts will be the most critical data upgrade in the final 24 hours.
  • The single variable that could most improve confidence: Monday morning TAFs and PIREPs over the Cascades confirming either frontal passage or IMC conditions. If those show clearing and post-frontal airmass established, confidence upgrades to Medium and the call improves.
